title: Doubly and circular lists

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Doubly and circular lists; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Doubly and circular lists
sources.claim: Doubly linked lists add previous links, while circular lists replace null ends with links forming a cycle and require stronger consistency invariants.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Doubly linked lists add previous links, while circular lists replace null ends with links forming a cycle and require stronger consistency invariants.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A circular traversal stops when current returns to head rather than when current becomes null. Your goal is to implement and test a doubly linked operation and explain circular termination conditions. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Array representation and operations; Insertion and deletion. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: Doubly linked lists add previous links, while circular lists replace null ends with links forming a cycle and require stronger consistency invariants. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, nodes need explicit allocation, links, and cleanup; in C++, prefer RAII ownership such as unique_ptr where it fits; in Java, node references are garbage-collected but links and equality remain explicit; in Python, object references simplify storage but do not change linear traversal complexity.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Extra links increase memory and update cases, and ordinary null-terminated traversal will not stop on a circular list. The draft therefore rejects: A circular list eventually reaches null after the tail like a singly linked list.

includes: Maintain bidirectional or cyclic invariants and compare added flexibility with added update risk.
Implement and test a doubly linked operation and explain circular termination conditions.

In C, nodes need explicit allocation, links, and cleanup; in C++, prefer RAII ownership such as unique_ptr where it fits; in Java, node references are garbage-collected but links and equality remain explicit; in Python, object references simplify storage but do not change linear traversal complexity.
excludes: Extra links increase memory and update cases, and ordinary null-terminated traversal will not stop on a circular list.

outcomes: Implement and test a doubly linked operation and explain circular termination conditions.

examples.title: Doubly and circular lists: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: A circular traversal stops when current returns to head rather than when current becomes null.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: A circular traversal stops when current returns to head rather than when current becomes null.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Circular traversal uses a sentinel or return-to-start condition, and every update preserves both forward and backward links.
examples.result: The conceptual result follows because Circular traversal uses a sentinel or return-to-start condition, and every update preserves both forward and backward links.
